C7-011.source_text:
7-2.10 REFERENCE LINE. When required, references must bear directly on the subject of the letter. List references in the order they appear in the text. Always mention cited references in the text. Never list the same item in both the reference and enclosure lines. If a reference is not held by all addressees, annotate the reference with the appropriate NOTAL statement. On copies only, not the original, annotate references with "w/ref (x)" where x is the reference letter.

Format the reference line as follows:
- Place "Ref:" on the second line below the subject line (one blank line between Subject and Ref:)
- Follow "Ref:" with four spaces
- Use lowercase letters in parentheses: (a), (b), (c), ..., (z), (aa), (ab), (ac), etc.
- Continuation lines align under the first word after "Ref:    "

Reference formats:
1. Naval correspondence: (a)  COMNAVAIRFORINST 5216.5A
2. Business letters: (b)  Smith Industries letter Ser 123/45 of 15 Mar 2025
3. E-mails: (c)  John Smith e-mail of 15 Mar 2025
4. Messages: (d)  COMNAVAIRLANT message 151000Z Mar 2025
5. Endorsements: (e)  NAVSUP 5216/123 of 15 Mar 2025
6. PHONCON/Mtg: (f)  PHONCON with Mr. John Smith on 15 Mar 2025 / (g)  Mtg with Mr. John Smith on 15 Mar 2025
7. Instructions: (h)  SECNAVINST 5216.5A
8. Notices: (i)  SECNAVNOTICE 5216
9. DoD directives: (j)  DoDD 5216.5
10. Forms: (k)  SECNAV Form 5216/1
11. Reports: (l)  Report of 15 Mar 2025
12. Publications: (m)  Navy Correspondence Manual
13. CFR: (n)  32 CFR 700
14. FR: (o)  89 FR 12345
15. U.S.C.: (p)  10 U.S.C. 7001
16. E.O.: (q)  E.O. 12345
17. My usage: (r)  My message of 15 Mar 2025
18. Your usage: (s)  Your message of 15 Mar 2025
C7-011.plain_language_rule: When references are required, they must directly relate to the letter's subject. List references in the exact order they first appear in the body text, assigning sequential markers (a), (b), (c), etc. as they appear. After (z), continue with (aa), (ab), (ac), and so on. Every reference listed must be explicitly mentioned in the text. The same item cannot appear in both reference and enclosure lines. Format: 'Ref:' on the second line below Subject (one blank line gap), followed by four spaces, then the reference markers. Continuation lines align under the first character after 'Ref:    '. If a reference is not held by all addressees, add a NOTAL annotation. On copies only (not the original), add 'w/ref (x)' where x is the reference letter.
C7-011.notes: Critical placement: Ref: appears on the second line below Subject with exactly one blank line between them. Spacing: exactly four spaces after 'Ref:' before the first marker. Marker sequencing: lowercase letters in parentheses, assigned sequentially as references appear in text (not alphabetically sorted by title). After (z) comes (aa), (ab), etc. Continuation lines align under the first word after the heading. Content requirement: 'Always mention cited references in the text' means every listed reference must be explicitly cited in the body. Ordering rule: 'List references in the order they appear in the text' means order of first mention in body text, with markers assigned sequentially. The alphabetical sequence (a), (b), (c)... is the marker system, not a sorting criterion. Enclosure exception: never duplicate an item in both reference and enclosure lines. NOTAL handling: annotate references not held by all addressees. w/ref (x) notation: appears on copies only, never on the original.

C7-012.source_text:
11. Enclosure Line.

a. General. List enclosures in the enclosure line in the order they appear in the text. Identify an enclosure using the same format as you would when identifying a reference. See paragraph 10 above. When identifying a document, cite its subject or title exactly. Never list an item in both the enclosure line and reference line of the same letter.

b. Format. Type "Encl:" at the left margin on the second line below the last line of the previous heading. Three spaces follow the colon. Use a number in parentheses before the description of every enclosure, even if you have only one. One space follows the closing parenthesis. If the entry is longer than one line, start the second line under the first word after the heading.

EXAMPLE:
Encl: (1) List of Reserve Officers Selected for Promotion to Colonel (2) CMC ltr 5216 Ser 00/451 of 5 Sep 09

c. Normal Distribution and When to Vary It. Normally, send one copy of the basic letter plus any enclosures to all addressees. Do not use the term "w/encl" to indicate that all addressees are being sent the enclosures. Avoid sending an enclosure if an addressee has it already or if its bulk or other factors make furnishing it impractical.

d. Adding Copies of Enclosures for all Addressees. When sending more than one copy of an enclosure to all addressees ("To," "Via," and "Copy to"), note the quantity in parentheses after the enclosure's description.

EXAMPLE: Encl: (1) OPNAV 5216/10 (10 Copies)

e. Variations Affecting Only Copy To Addressees. In certain circumstances, it may be necessary to vary the normal distribution of enclosures to "Copy to" addressees. It is up to the originator of the correspondence to make this determination. There are two basic methods of varying distribution; all "Copy to" addressees are affected or only one or a few of the "Copy to" addressees are affected.

(1) In these examples, all "Copy to" addressees are affected in the same way, so notes appear beside the "Copy to" heading.

EXAMPLE:
Copy to: (w/o encls) Copy to: (w/o encls (2) and (3)) Copy to: (w/2 copies of encl (1))

(2) In this example, only some of the "Copy to" addressees are affected, so notes appear beside the affected addressees.

EXAMPLE:
Copy to: COMNAVSURFPAC (N1) (w/o encls) USS MUSTIN (w/encl (2) only) USS VANDERGRIFT

f. Variations Affecting Only Via Addressees. When varying the normal distribution of enclosures to "Via" addressees, show the variation beside the affected addressee. One possible variation appears below. Others may be adapted for the examples in paragraph 11e above.

EXAMPLE:
Via: Commanding Officer, Naval Technical Training Center, Meridian (w/o encl)

g. Sending Enclosures Separately. When size, weight, or other factors prevent sending an enclosure with a letter, send it separately and type the term "sep cover" in parentheses after the enclosure's description.

EXAMPLE:
Encl: (1) SECNAVINST 5211.5E (sep cover)
C7-012.plain_language_rule: List enclosures in the exact order they first appear in the body text. Identify each enclosure using the same format as references (see C7-011). Cite the subject or title exactly as it appears. Never list the same item in both the enclosure line and reference line of the same letter. Format: 'Encl:' at left margin on the second line below the previous heading, followed by three spaces. Use numbers in parentheses (1), (2), (3), etc. before each enclosure description, even for a single enclosure. One space follows the closing parenthesis. Continuation lines align under the first word after the heading. Normally send one copy of the letter plus enclosures to all addressees; do not use 'w/encl' to indicate this. Avoid sending enclosures if the addressee already has them or if bulk makes it impractical. When sending multiple copies of an enclosure to all addressees, note the quantity in parentheses after the description. Distribution variations for 'Copy to' and 'Via' addressees can be noted beside the heading or beside specific addressees. When an enclosure is sent separately due to size or weight, add '(sep cover)' after the description.
C7-012.notes: Critical placement: Encl: appears on the second line below the previous heading (Ref: or Subject: if no Ref). Spacing: exactly three spaces after 'Encl:' before the first marker. Marker format: numbers in parentheses (1), (2), (3)... not letters. One space follows the closing parenthesis before the description. Continuation lines align under the first word after the heading. Ordering rule: 'order they appear in the text' means order of first mention in body text. Format requirement: identify enclosures using same format as references (C7-011). Duplicate prohibition: never list same item in both enclosure and reference lines of the same letter. Distribution: normal is one copy to all addressees; do not annotate with 'w/encl'. Multiple copies: note quantity as '(10 Copies)' after description. Variation notation: 'w/o encls', 'w/encl (x) only', 'w/2 copies of encl (1)' appear beside 'Copy to:' heading or beside specific addressees. Separate mailing: use '(sep cover)' parenthetical after description when enclosure is mailed separately.

C7-013.source_text:
12. Text. Start the text on the second line down from the previous entry. The text shall be left justified. Make the content clear by using plain English. Do not use slang or jargon. Refer to chapter 12 for guidance on writing. Within the text, spell out all acronyms the first time used. After first use, acronyms shall be used throughout the rest of the correspondence.

13. Paragraphs. Start all continuation lines at the left margin. All paragraphs are single spaced and each paragraph or subparagraph begins on the second line below the previous paragraph or subparagraph. When using a subparagraph, the first line is always indented the appropriate number of spaces depending on the level of subparagraphing. All other lines of a subparagraph continue at the left margin. Do not indent the continuation lines of a subparagraph. If there is a paragraph 1a, there must be a paragraph 1b; if there is a paragraph 1a(1), there must be a paragraph 1a(2), etc. It is acceptable for a paragraph to break across pages, but do not begin a paragraph at the bottom of a page unless there is enough space for at least two lines of text on that page and at least two lines of text are carried over to the next page. A signature page must have at least two lines of text preceding the signature.

a. Identifying Paragraphs or Subparagraphs. Identify all paragraphs or subparagraphs with a number or letter. See figure 7-8 for an example of paragraph and subparagraph structure and identification.

b. Limit Subparagraphs. Documents rarely require subdividing to the extent shown in figure 7-8. Do not subdivide past the second level until you have exhausted all re-paragraphing alternatives first. Never subparagraph beyond the levels shown in figure 7-8.

c. Citing Paragraphs. When citing a paragraph or subparagraph, write the numbers and letters without periods or spaces.
Example: 2b(4)(a)

d. Paragraph Headings. Use paragraph headings in long correspondence with widely varying topics. Be brief but informative. Underline any heading and capitalize its key words using the Title Case format. Be consistent across main paragraphs and subparagraphs. If paragraph 1 has a heading, then paragraph 2 would need a heading; if paragraph 1a has a heading, then paragraph 1b would need a heading.
C7-013.plain_language_rule: Start body text on the second line down from the previous entry. Text must be left justified (not justified). Write clear, plain English without slang or jargon. Spell out all acronyms at first use, then use the acronym throughout. Paragraph structure: start all continuation lines at the left margin (no hanging indents). All paragraphs are single-spaced. Each paragraph or subparagraph begins on the second line below the previous paragraph/subparagraph. Subparagraphs: first line indented (amount depends on level), all continuation lines at left margin. No hanging indents on continuation lines. Paragraph numbering: if paragraph 1a exists, must have 1b; if 1a(1) exists, must have 1a(2), etc. Paragraphs may break across pages, but never begin a paragraph at the bottom of a page unless there is room for at least two lines on that page AND at least two lines carried to the next. Signature page: must have at least two lines of text before signature. Identify all paragraphs/subparagraphs with numbers or letters. Limit subdivision to two levels maximum before exhausting all re-paragraphing alternatives. Use paragraph headings for long correspondence with widely varying topics. Underline headings and capitalize key words using Title Case. Be consistent across main paragraphs and subparagraphs.
C7-013.notes: Body text placement: start on second line down from previous entry (Ref:, Encl:, or signature). Justification: left justified (not justified). Content quality: plain English, no slang or jargon. Acronym handling: spell out at first use, then use acronym thereafter. Paragraph spacing: all paragraphs single-spaced. Paragraph/subparagraph placement: each begins on second line below previous one. Continuation lines: always at left margin (no hanging indents for continuation lines). Subparagraph structure: first line indented (amount depends on level), all other lines at left margin. Numbering requirement: sequential (1a must have 1b, 1a(1) must have 1a(2), etc.). Page breaks: paragraphs may break across pages, but never start paragraph at bottom unless room for 2+ lines on current page AND 2+ lines on next page. Signature page requirement: at least 2 lines of text before signature. Paragraph identification: use numbers or letters. Subparagraph limits: rarely need subdivision beyond second level. Paragraph headings: use for long correspondence with varying topics, underline and Title Case key words, be consistent.
